title: AWS Marketplace offer preflight playbook (2026): launch checklist + hero copy templates
preview:
h1. Purpose
This playbook is the standard preflight packet for an AWS Marketplace offer (new listing or major revision). It covers:
- The required artifacts to unblock Procurement/Legal review
- Recommended SKU structure and metering defaults
- Approved hero copy templates (with compliance-safe language)
- A one-page co-sell blurb template for AWS Partner Ops

This page was created after repeated partner asks for a “short checklist + 2–3 hero copy options” to speed internal alignment.

h1. When to use
Use this playbook when:
- A partner requests an AWS Marketplace listing (public listing, private offer, or both)
- A deal is targeting AWS co-sell registration
- Procurement requests a “preflight checklist” before engaging Legal/Security

h1. Owners and turnaround expectations
- DRI (Partners/Marketplace): Partnerships Director (default)
- Messaging: Product Marketing
- Packaging/Metering: Platform GTM + Product
- Co-sell: Partner Ops enablement

SLA targets (typical):
1) Preflight checklist + draft hero copy within 2 business days
2) Consolidated partner packet within 3 business days

h1. Preflight packet contents (send as one consolidated PDF or folder)
Deliverables to include:
1) Offer preflight checklist (see below)
2) Hero copy options (2–3) + subheadline
3) SKU + metering summary (1 page)
4) Co-sell one-pager blurb (paragraph + value props + use cases)
5) Image guidance notes (or link to latest design guidance)

h1. Offer preflight checklist (required artifacts)
The table below is the canonical “what we need” list. If a partner asks for a checklist, copy/paste this section into email and offer to provide a filled-in version.

h2. Checklist table
| Category | Item | Required for | Notes / tips |
|---|---|---|---|
| Listing basics | Product name + short description | All offers | Keep description consistent with website naming; avoid unverified superlatives. |
| Listing basics | Category + keywords | All offers | Use stable keyword set across AWS + website + decks. |
| Packaging | SKU names + SKU descriptions | All offers | See recommended SKU set below; keep names stable for billing mappings. |
| Metering | Metering dimension(s) + unit definitions | SaaS offers | Prefer token-based metering where applicable; define rounding/minimums explicitly. |
| Pricing | Public price card | Public listing | Include any volume tiers and minimum monthly commits if applicable. |
| Pricing | Private Offer readiness | Enterprise deals | Confirm discount bands and approval path; define start/end dates and renewal term language. |
| Legal | EULA/contract addendum link | All offers | Confirm latest template; avoid partner-specific edits without Legal review. |
| Security | SOC 2 / ISO status statement | Most enterprise deals | Provide the “what we can share” summary and the evidence request process. |
| Support | Support tiers + response times | All offers | Provide business hours, P1 expectations, and escalation channel. |
| Images | Hero image(s) + logo usage approval | Public listing | Follow AWS image constraints; do not use customer logos without written permission. |
| Sales enablement | Value props (top 3) + ICP | Co-sell | Must map to measurable outcomes; avoid hard performance numbers unless approved. |
| Implementation | Getting started steps + prerequisites | All offers | Include IAM/network prerequisites and onboarding timeline ranges. |

h1. SKU and metering guidance (2026 standard)
This section supersedes older notes that used different metering for dedicated capacity.

h2. Recommended SKU set
Default recommendation is 3 SKUs (use 2 only if product scope is narrow):
- Standard (Shared hosted): best for trials, dev/test, and smaller workloads
- Dedicated (Reserved capacity): best for predictable production workloads needing isolation
- Enterprise (Private Offer only): used when commercial terms require custom commitments, add-ons, or bundled support

Important: Some partners will ask whether “standard/dedicated” is final so their billing team can map the catalog. For 2026, these names are the preferred defaults; only change if there is an existing contracted naming scheme.

h2. Metering and pricing defaults
- Primary usage metric (preferred): tokens-in/tokens-out (or “total tokens processed” if a single dimension is required)
- Dedicated capacity: prefer token-based metering plus a minimum monthly commit rather than vGPU-hour metering, unless Finance explicitly requires capacity-based billing for that product line
- Pricing footnotes to include:
  1) Clear definition of what counts as input vs output tokens
  2) Rounding behavior and any minimum billable unit
  3) Overages/burst behavior (if enabled)

h2. Private Offer guidance
- Default: enable Private Offer for initial enterprise customers to accelerate procurement and ensure predictable billing
- Include in partner packet:
  - Proposed term (12/24/36 months)
  - Discount band (if pre-approved)
  - Start date, renewal behavior, and any ramp schedule

h1. Hero copy templates (compliance-safe)
Keep hero copy short (target: <= 85 characters for the main line). Avoid unapproved hard claims (exact latency, “X% cheaper”) unless Product + Legal sign-off is recorded.

h2. Options (2026)
Option A — "LLM inference, production-grade: predictable latency and cost at scale."
Subline — "Run open and custom models—fast, reliable, and cloud-ready."

Option B — "Ship AI features with zero ops friction: high-throughput inference for teams."
Subline — "Dedicated capacity, private options, and integrated cost controls."

Option C — "Cut inference costs. Keep quality. Scale confidently."
Subline — "Unified runtime across hosted, dedicated, and private deployments."

Notes:
- Option B text reflects the preferred legal-friendly wording (“with zero ops friction”) and replaces older phrasing (“without the ops”).
- If a partner requests stronger performance language, route to Product for claim substantiation and approval.

h1. Co-sell blurb template (AWS Partner Ops)
Provide AWS Partner Ops a single paragraph plus 3 value props and 3 use cases.

h2. One-paragraph template
"Redwood Inference helps teams ship production AI features by providing reliable, cost-controlled model inference across shared hosted and dedicated capacity. Customers use Redwood to reduce operational overhead, improve predictability for latency-sensitive workloads, and scale usage with clear commercial terms through AWS Marketplace."

h2. Top 3 value props (edit to fit product scope)
- Predictable performance for production inference workloads
- Cost controls and transparent metering aligned to usage
- Flexible deployment options (shared, dedicated, private offer commercial paths)

h2. Top 3 use cases (choose the most relevant)
- Agentic workflows and RAG applications in customer support and knowledge search
- Real-time personalization and recommendation inference
- Internal copilots and document automation for operations teams

h1. Email-ready response (copy/paste)
Subject: AWS Marketplace preflight pack: checklist + hero copy + SKU guidance

Body:
"Thanks—attached is our AWS Marketplace offer preflight pack. It includes (1) the required artifacts checklist, (2) 3 hero copy options with sublines, (3) recommended SKU/metering defaults, and (4) a co-sell one-paragraph summary + value props/use cases. If Procurement/Legal needs MSA redlines or security evidence, tell us what format they prefer and we’ll prioritize."

h1. Change log
- 2026-12-10: Updated Option B hero copy to “with zero ops friction” and clarified SKU/metering default to prefer token-based metering for dedicated offers unless Finance requires capacity-based billing.
- 2026-11-05: Initial publication based on partner request pattern (checklist + 2–3 hero copy options + SKU guidance + co-sell blurb).

title: Runbook: Suggestions API Latency and 5xx Triage
preview:
## Purpose
This runbook covers triage and mitigation for elevated latency and/or 5xx error rate on the **Suggestions API** used by the Redwood Console “Optimization suggestions” beta.

Primary goals:
- Restore availability and latency SLOs quickly.
- Prevent cross-tenant or broad Console impact (UI timeouts, repeated retries).
- Preserve correctness guarantees (RBAC + org/project scoping) while degraded.

Non-goals:
- Deep root cause analysis (capture data for follow-up; open an incident or ticket as needed).

---

## Service / Endpoint Overview
**User-facing surface:** Redwood Console → Optimize → Suggestions

**Backend service:** `optimize-suggestions-service`

**Primary endpoint (internal naming):**
- `GET /optimize/suggestions` (list; paginated, sortable)
- `GET /optimize/suggestions/{suggestion_id}` (details)

**Common query parameters:**
- `org_id`, `project_id`
- `route_id` (optional filter)
- `model` (optional filter)
- `sort` (e.g., `estimated_savings_usd_day_desc`, `risk_asc`)
- `page_size`, `page_token`

**Critical dependencies:**
- Suggestions DB (Postgres)
- Cache layer (Redis) for hot list requests (if enabled)
- RBAC/Authz service and policy store
- Suggestions compute pipeline (produces precomputed estimates + `stale_as_of`)

---

## SLOs, Alerts, and What “Bad” Looks Like
### SLO targets (beta)
These are operational targets for the **API**, not the compute pipeline:
- **Availability:** 99.9% (per 30d) for `GET /optimize/suggestions`
- **Latency:** p95 < 350ms for list endpoint (typical page_size 25)
- **Error rate:** 5xx < 0.2% (5-minute window)

### Common alert triggers
- `SuggestionsAPIHighP95Latency` (p95 > 500ms for 10m)
- `SuggestionsAPI5xxRateHigh` (5xx > 1% for 5m)
- `SuggestionsAPITimeouts` (upstream gateway timeouts elevated)
- `SuggestionsDBPoolSaturation` (connection pool > 90% for 10m)

### User symptoms
- Console Suggestions page spinner/timeouts
- Empty states that look like errors (e.g., “Couldn’t load suggestions”)
- Rapid retries from Console amplifying load

---

## Where to Look First (Dashboards)
1) **Grafana: Suggestions API Health (Overview)**
- Panels: RPS, p50/p95 latency, 4xx/5xx, timeouts, payload size
- Breakdowns: region, cluster, endpoint, status_code
- Link (internal): `grafana://dashboards/suggestions-api-overview`

2) **Grafana: Suggestions API – Dependency Drilldown**
- DB query latency, DB connections, Redis ops/latency, authz latency
- Link (internal): `grafana://dashboards/suggestions-api-deps`

3) **Grafana: Suggestions Compute Lag**
- Compute job success rate, last successful run, lag minutes, stale rows
- Link (internal): `grafana://dashboards/suggestions-compute-lag`

4) **Funnel dashboard (for blast radius / retries)**
- Impressions → expand → error events
- Link (internal): `grafana://dashboards/suggestions-beta-funnel`

---

## Fast Triage Checklist (10 minutes)
### Step 0 — Confirm scope and severity
- Is it **one region/cluster** or global?
- Is it **list endpoint only** or also details?
- Is the issue **latency**, **5xx**, or both?
- Is there a correlated **deploy**, **config change**, or **feature flag ramp**?

### Step 1 — Check recent changes
- Deploys (last 2 hours): `optimize-suggestions-service`, authz service, DB migrations
- Console rollout: Suggestions feature flag ramp, cohort expansion
- Infra: DB failover, Redis maintenance, network incidents

### Step 2 — Identify dominant failure mode
Use dashboard splits by `status_code` and error class.
- If **5xx spikes** with stable latency → likely exceptions, dependency outage, pool exhaustion
- If **p95 latency climbs** with rising DB time → query regression or DB contention
- If **gateway timeouts** dominate → threads blocked, deadlocks, slow queries, downstream timeouts

### Step 3 — Capture evidence for follow-up
- Snapshot graphs (latency, 5xx, DB latency, DB pool)
- Top error messages (from logs)
- A few example request IDs / trace IDs

---

## Logs, Traces, and Key Signals
### Logs (Kibana/Loki)
Filter by:
- `service=optimize-suggestions-service`
- `endpoint=/optimize/suggestions`
- `status>=500`

Look for:
- DB errors: `too many connections`, `timeout`, `deadlock detected`
- Authz errors: policy fetch timeouts
- Serialization/payload errors: response too large
- Pagination token parsing failures (can present as 5xx if unhandled)

### Traces (Tempo/Honeycomb)
Find slow traces and identify which span dominates:
- `db.query` (slow SQL)
- `authz.check` (RBAC latency)
- `redis.get`/`redis.mget` (cache slowness)

### Metrics (names may vary by cluster)
- `http_server_request_duration_seconds{service="optimize-suggestions-service"}`
- `http_server_requests_total{status_code=~"5.."}`
- `db_pool_in_use{service="optimize-suggestions-service"}`
- `db_query_duration_seconds{query_name=...}`
- `authz_request_duration_seconds`
- `redis_command_duration_seconds{command=...}`

---

## Common Causes and Mitigations
### A) DB query regression / N+1 behavior
**Signals**
- p95 increases with DB time; CPU on DB rises; slow query log shows repeated similar queries.
- Latency worsens with higher `page_size`.

**Immediate mitigations**
1) Reduce load:
- Temporarily reduce Console page_size defaults (if configurable via server config).
- Consider throttling list endpoint at the gateway for abusive orgs (avoid global throttles if possible).

2) Enable/Increase caching for list responses (if safe for RBAC scoping):
- Ensure cache key includes `org_id`, `project_id`, filters, and `sort`.
- Keep TTL short (e.g., 60–120s) to avoid confusion and preserve “near-real-time” expectation.

3) If a known regression is identified, roll back:
- Roll back the last deploy to `optimize-suggestions-service`.
- If caused by a query change, prefer rollback over hotfix unless rollback is blocked.

**Follow-up**
- Open ENG ticket and attach the slow query / trace evidence.
- If this resembles the prior N+1 pattern, reference the query optimization work (e.g., “N+1 in suggestions query” fix).

---

### B) DB connection pool saturation
**Signals**
- Elevated 5xx with errors like `remaining connection slots are reserved`.
- `db_pool_in_use` near max; queue wait time increases.

**Immediate mitigations**
1) Scale application pods (carefully):
- Scaling pods can worsen DB saturation if pool sizes scale linearly.
- If scaling, ensure max connections per pod is bounded.

2) Reduce concurrent work:
- Temporarily lower worker concurrency / thread pool if requests are blocking.

3) Gate traffic:
- Pause new beta org enables (feature flag) and/or reduce cohorts.

4) Engage DB on-call if the DB itself is overloaded (high CPU/IO, long locks).

**Escalation trigger**
- Pool saturation sustained > 15m or repeated DB connection errors → page SRE + DB owner.

---

### C) Redis/cache degradation (if enabled)
**Signals**
- `redis_command_duration` increases; timeouts; cache miss rate spikes.
- Latency increases but DB time may remain flat (if app blocks on cache timeouts).

**Immediate mitigations**
1) Fail open on cache:
- Switch cache operations to non-blocking mode / shorter timeouts (config).
- If Redis is causing tail latency, temporarily disable cache reads (keep writes off as well).

2) Reduce cache key cardinality:
- Ensure keys are not including high-cardinality fields unnecessarily.

---

### D) Authz/RBAC dependency latency
**Signals**
- Traces show `authz.check` dominating.
- Increased 5xx due to authz timeouts or circuit breaker open.

**Immediate mitigations**
1) Confirm we are not making per-row authz checks.
- List endpoint should authorize at org/project scope once, then filter results.

2) Enable authz result caching (short TTL) if available.

3) If authz is down: fail closed (no data) rather than serving suggestions without verification.
- Prefer returning a 403/feature unavailable rather than leaking data.

---

### E) Suggestions compute lag / stale data confusion (not usually 5xx)
Compute lag typically causes “stale_as_of” to be old or “insufficient telemetry” states, but can cause backend errors if invariants break.

**Signals**
- Compute lag dashboard shows last successful run too old.
- Errors referencing missing aggregates or null estimate fields.

**Immediate mitigations**
1) Degrade to “stale but serveable”:
- Continue serving last known suggestions with `stale_as_of` intact.
- Do not recompute synchronously on read.

2) Pause compute backfills if they overload the DB.
- Prefer off-peak backfills.

3) If compute job is failing repeatedly:
- Page compute owner (Optimize pipeline) and consider disabling new suggestion generation while serving cached results.

---

## Safe Degradation Options (Preferred Order)
Use these to reduce user impact while protecting safety and trust.

1) **Serve cached / precomputed results with explicit staleness**
- Keep `stale_as_of` visible to UI.

2) **Return partial list with clear UI messaging**
- Example: “Some filters are unavailable right now. Showing top suggestions only.”

3) **Temporarily disable expensive fields** (only if the API contract allows)
- Example: omit confidence bands or verbose explanations if they cause payload bloat.
- Must maintain backward compatibility per API contract.

4) **Feature flag off** for impacted cohort
- If needed, disable Suggestions UI entry point for newly-enabled orgs first.

5) **Hard disable endpoint** (last resort)
- Return 503 with Retry-After and ensure Console handles gracefully.

---

## Step-by-Step: What to Do During an Active Alert
### 1) Acknowledge and assess
- Confirm alert is real (not a monitor glitch).
- Identify affected clusters/regions.

### 2) Stabilize
- If 5xx > 5% or timeouts are high: reduce traffic first (pause cohort ramp, throttle abusive orgs).
- If p95 is high with DB saturation: prioritize DB protection (reduce concurrency, consider rollback).

### 3) Identify top contributor
- Check traces for the slowest span type.
- Check logs for top error signature.

### 4) Apply the smallest reversible mitigation
- Prefer config toggles and rollbacks over code changes during incident.

### 5) Validate improvement
- Watch latency p95 and 5xx over 10–15 minutes.
- Confirm no new regression (e.g., 4xx spike from authz failures).

### 6) Communicate
- Update incident channel with:
  - Current status, suspected cause
  - Mitigation applied
  - Next checkpoint time

---

## Rollback / Feature Flag Procedures
### Backend deploy rollback
- Use standard deploy tooling to roll back `optimize-suggestions-service` to last known good.
- Confirm schema compatibility (no forward-only migrations).

### Console feature flag gating
- If Console traffic is the amplifier, disable Suggestions view for new cohorts first.
- Keep internal dogfood enabled if it’s needed for debugging and is low volume.

Audit expectations:
- Record org IDs enabled/disabled and reason.
- Ensure access changes are logged (especially if involving RBAC scopes).

---

## Escalation Paths
Page in this order, based on evidence:

1) **Eng Platform On-call (API owner)**
- Primary responder for API errors, deployment, caches.

2) **SRE On-call**
- If multi-service incident, gateway timeouts, cluster instability, or broad customer impact.

3) **DB On-call / Infra**
- If DB saturation, lock contention, failover, or disk/IO pressure.

4) **Telemetry/Compute owners**
- If compute lag/failure is causing API exceptions or overload (backfills).

When to declare an incident:
- User-facing 5xx > 1% sustained > 10m for multiple orgs, OR
- p95 > 2s sustained > 15m, OR
- Any suspicion of cross-tenant data risk (immediate incident + security escalation).

---

## Post-Incident Follow-ups (Required)
Within 24 hours of stabilization:
- Create a ticket with:
  - Start/end time, regions affected
  - Primary metrics (p95, 5xx, timeouts)
  - Root cause hypothesis and evidence (logs/traces/screenshots)
  - Mitigation applied and any downsides
  - Concrete prevention items (query fix, caching changes, monitor tuning)

Within 5 business days (if severity warrants):
- Write an incident review summary (or attach to ongoing reliability initiative).

---

## Appendix: Quick Commands
### cURL sanity checks (internal)
- List:
  - `curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" "https://api.redwood.example/optimize/suggestions?org_id=...&project_id=...&page_size=25" -v`
- Details:
  - `curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" "https://api.redwood.example/optimize/suggestions/$SUGGESTION_ID" -v`

What to verify:
- Server timing, status code
- Response size
- Presence of `stale_as_of` and stable `suggestion_id`

### kubectl (if applicable)
- Pods:
  - `kubectl -n optimize get pods -l app=optimize-suggestions-service`
- Recent errors:
  - `kubectl -n optimize logs deploy/optimize-suggestions-service --since=10m | grep -E "ERROR|panic|timeout"`

---

## Appendix: Known Edge Cases
- Large orgs with many routes/models can produce heavy list queries if filtering is absent.
- Certain sorts (e.g., sorting by computed savings fields) can be expensive if not indexed.
- Page token parsing errors can cascade into 5xx if not handled defensively.
- Retries from Console can amplify load; watch for sudden RPS step functions correlated with UI changes.

If any of the above is suspected, prefer mitigation via throttling + rollback, then follow up with indexing/query fixes and contract-safe caching.

**Critical dependencies:**
- Suggestions DB (Postgres)
- Cache layer (Redis) for hot list requests (if enabled)
- RBAC/Authz service and policy store
- Suggestions compute pipeline (produces precomputed estimates + `stale_as_of`)

---

## SLOs, Alerts, and What “Bad” Looks Like
### SLO targets (beta)
These are operational targets for the **API**, not the compute pipeline:
- **Availability:** 99.9% (per 30d) for `GET /optimize/suggestions`
- **Latency:** p95 < 350ms for list endpoint (typical page_size 25)
- **Error rate:** 5xx < 0.2% (5-minute window)

### Common alert triggers
- `SuggestionsAPIHighP95Latency` (p95 > 500ms for 10m)
- `SuggestionsAPI5xxRateHigh` (5xx > 1% for 5m)
- `SuggestionsAPITimeouts` (upstream gateway timeouts elevated)
- `SuggestionsDBPoolSaturation` (connection pool > 90% for 10m)

### User symptoms
- Console Suggestions page spinner/timeouts
- Empty states that look like errors (e.g., “Couldn’t load suggestions”)
- Rapid retries from Console amplifying load

---

## Where to Look First (Dashboards)
1) **Grafana: Suggestions API Health (Overview)**
- Panels: RPS, p50/p95 latency, 4xx/5xx, timeouts, payload size
- Breakdowns: region, cluster, endpoint, status_code
- Link (internal): `grafana://dashboards/suggestions-api-overview`

2) **Grafana: Suggestions API – Dependency Drilldown**
- DB query latency, DB connections, Redis ops/latency, authz latency
- Link (internal): `grafana://dashboards/suggestions-api-deps`

3) **Grafana: Suggestions Compute Lag**
- Compute job success rate, last successful run, lag minutes, stale rows
- Link (internal): `grafana://dashboards/suggestions-compute-lag`

4) **Funnel dashboard (for blast radius / retries)**
- Impressions → expand → error events
- Link (internal): `grafana://dashboards/suggestions-beta-funnel`

---

## Fast Triage Checklist (10 minutes)
### Step 0 — Confirm scope and severity
- Is it **one region/cluster** or global?
- Is it **list endpoint only** or also details?
- Is the issue **latency**, **5xx**, or both?
- Is there a correlated **deploy**, **config change**, or **feature flag ramp**?

### Step 1 — Check recent changes
- Deploys (last 2 hours): `optimize-suggestions-service`, authz service, DB migrations
- Console rollout: Suggestions feature flag ramp, cohort expansion
- Infra: DB failover, Redis maintenance, network incidents

### Step 2 — Identify dominant failure mode
Use dashboard splits by `status_code` and error class.
- If **5xx spikes** with stable latency → likely exceptions, dependency outage, pool exhaustion
- If **p95 latency climbs** with rising DB time → query regression or DB contention
- If **gateway timeouts** dominate → threads blocked, deadlocks, slow queries, downstream timeouts

### Step 3 — Capture evidence for follow-up
- Snapshot graphs (latency, 5xx, DB latency, DB pool)
- Top error messages (from logs)
- A few example request IDs / trace IDs

---

## Logs, Traces, and Key Signals
### Logs (Kibana/Loki)
Filter by:
- `service=optimize-suggestions-service`
- `endpoint=/optimize/suggestions`
- `status>=500`

Look for:
- DB errors: `too many connections`, `timeout`, `deadlock detected`
- Authz errors: policy fetch timeouts
- Serialization/payload errors: response too large
- Pagination token parsing failures (can present as 5xx if unhandled)

### Traces (Tempo/Honeycomb)
Find slow traces and identify which span dominates:
- `db.query` (slow SQL)
- `authz.check` (RBAC latency)
- `redis.get`/`redis.mget` (cache slowness)

### Metrics (names may vary by cluster)
- `http_server_request_duration_seconds{service="optimize-suggestions-service"}`
- `http_server_requests_total{status_code=~"5.."}`
- `db_pool_in_use{service="optimize-suggestions-service"}`
- `db_query_duration_seconds{query_name=...}`
- `authz_request_duration_seconds`
- `redis_command_duration_seconds{command=...}`

---

## Common Causes and Mitigations
### A) DB query regression / N+1 behavior
**Signals**
- p95 increases with DB time; CPU on DB rises; slow query log shows repeated similar queries.
- Latency worsens with higher `page_size`.

**Immediate mitigations**
1) Reduce load:
- Temporarily reduce Console page_size defaults (if configurable via server config).
- Consider throttling list endpoint at the gateway for abusive orgs (avoid global throttles if possible).

2) Enable/Increase caching for list responses (if safe for RBAC scoping):
- Ensure cache key includes `org_id`, `project_id`, filters, and `sort`.
- Keep TTL short (e.g., 60–120s) to avoid confusion and preserve “near-real-time” expectation.

3) If a known regression is identified, roll back:
- Roll back the last deploy to `optimize-suggestions-service`.
- If caused by a query change, prefer rollback over hotfix unless rollback is blocked.

**Follow-up**
- Open ENG ticket and attach the slow query / trace evidence.
- If this resembles the prior N+1 pattern, reference the query optimization work (e.g., “N+1 in suggestions query” fix).

---

### B) DB connection pool saturation
**Signals**
- Elevated 5xx with errors like `remaining connection slots are reserved`.
- `db_pool_in_use` near max; queue wait time increases.

**Immediate mitigations**
1) Scale application pods (carefully):
- Scaling pods can worsen DB saturation if pool sizes scale linearly.
- If scaling, ensure max connections per pod is bounded.

2) Reduce concurrent work:
- Temporarily lower worker concurrency / thread pool if requests are blocking.

3) Gate traffic:
- Pause new beta org enables (feature flag) and/or reduce cohorts.

4) Engage DB on-call if the DB itself is overloaded (high CPU/IO, long locks).

**Escalation trigger**
- Pool saturation sustained > 15m or repeated DB connection errors → page SRE + DB owner.

---

### C) Redis/cache degradation (if enabled)
**Signals**
- `redis_command_duration` increases; timeouts; cache miss rate spikes.
- Latency increases but DB time may remain flat (if app blocks on cache timeouts).

**Immediate mitigations**
1) Fail open on cache:
- Switch cache operations to non-blocking mode / shorter timeouts (config).
- If Redis is causing tail latency, temporarily disable cache reads (keep writes off as well).

2) Reduce cache key cardinality:
- Ensure keys are not including high-cardinality fields unnecessarily.

---

### D) Authz/RBAC dependency latency
**Signals**
- Traces show `authz.check` dominating.
- Increased 5xx due to authz timeouts or circuit breaker open.

**Immediate mitigations**
1) Confirm we are not making per-row authz checks.
- List endpoint should authorize at org/project scope once, then filter results.

2) Enable authz result caching (short TTL) if available.

3) If authz is down: fail closed (no data) rather than serving suggestions without verification.
- Prefer returning a 403/feature unavailable rather than leaking data.

---

### E) Suggestions compute lag / stale data confusion (not usually 5xx)
Compute lag typically causes “stale_as_of” to be old or “insufficient telemetry” states, but can cause backend errors if invariants break.

**Signals**
- Compute lag dashboard shows last successful run too old.
- Errors referencing missing aggregates or null estimate fields.

**Immediate mitigations**
1) Degrade to “stale but serveable”:
- Continue serving last known suggestions with `stale_as_of` intact.
- Do not recompute synchronously on read.

2) Pause compute backfills if they overload the DB.
- Prefer off-peak backfills.

3) If compute job is failing repeatedly:
- Page compute owner (Optimize pipeline) and consider disabling new suggestion generation while serving cached results.

---

## Safe Degradation Options (Preferred Order)
Use these to reduce user impact while protecting safety and trust.

1) **Serve cached / precomputed results with explicit staleness**
- Keep `stale_as_of` visible to UI.

2) **Return partial list with clear UI messaging**
- Example: “Some filters are unavailable right now. Showing top suggestions only.”

3) **Temporarily disable expensive fields** (only if the API contract allows)
- Example: omit confidence bands or verbose explanations if they cause payload bloat.
- Must maintain backward compatibility per API contract.

4) **Feature flag off** for impacted cohort
- If needed, disable Suggestions UI entry point for newly-enabled orgs first.

5) **Hard disable endpoint** (last resort)
- Return 503 with Retry-After and ensure Console handles gracefully.

---

## Step-by-Step: What to Do During an Active Alert
### 1) Acknowledge and assess
- Confirm alert is real (not a monitor glitch).
- Identify affected clusters/regions.

### 2) Stabilize
- If 5xx > 5% or timeouts are high: reduce traffic first (pause cohort ramp, throttle abusive orgs).
- If p95 is high with DB saturation: prioritize DB protection (reduce concurrency, consider rollback).

### 3) Identify top contributor
- Check traces for the slowest span type.
- Check logs for top error signature.

### 4) Apply the smallest reversible mitigation
- Prefer config toggles and rollbacks over code changes during incident.

### 5) Validate improvement
- Watch latency p95 and 5xx over 10–15 minutes.
- Confirm no new regression (e.g., 4xx spike from authz failures).

### 6) Communicate
- Update incident channel with:
  - Current status, suspected cause
  - Mitigation applied
  - Next checkpoint time

---

## Rollback / Feature Flag Procedures
### Backend deploy rollback
- Use standard deploy tooling to roll back `optimize-suggestions-service` to last known good.
- Confirm schema compatibility (no forward-only migrations).

### Console feature flag gating
- If Console traffic is the amplifier, disable Suggestions view for new cohorts first.
- Keep internal dogfood enabled if it’s needed for debugging and is low volume.

Audit expectations:
- Record org IDs enabled/disabled and reason.
- Ensure access changes are logged (especially if involving RBAC scopes).

---

## Escalation Paths
Page in this order, based on evidence:

1) **Eng Platform On-call (API owner)**
- Primary responder for API errors, deployment, caches.

2) **SRE On-call**
- If multi-service incident, gateway timeouts, cluster instability, or broad customer impact.

3) **DB On-call / Infra**
- If DB saturation, lock contention, failover, or disk/IO pressure.

4) **Telemetry/Compute owners**
- If compute lag/failure is causing API exceptions or overload (backfills).

When to declare an incident:
- User-facing 5xx > 1% sustained > 10m for multiple orgs, OR
- p95 > 2s sustained > 15m, OR
- Any suspicion of cross-tenant data risk (immediate incident + security escalation).

---

## Post-Incident Follow-ups (Required)
Within 24 hours of stabilization:
- Create a ticket with:
  - Start/end time, regions affected
  - Primary metrics (p95, 5xx, timeouts)
  - Root cause hypothesis and evidence (logs/traces/screenshots)
  - Mitigation applied and any downsides
  - Concrete prevention items (query fix, caching changes, monitor tuning)

Within 5 business days (if severity warrants):
- Write an incident review summary (or attach to ongoing reliability initiative).

---

## Appendix: Quick Commands
### cURL sanity checks (internal)
- List:
  - `curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" "https://api.redwood.example/optimize/suggestions?org_id=...&project_id=...&page_size=25" -v`
- Details:
  - `curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" "https://api.redwood.example/optimize/suggestions/$SUGGESTION_ID" -v`

What to verify:
- Server timing, status code
- Response size
- Presence of `stale_as_of` and stable `suggestion_id`

### kubectl (if applicable)
- Pods:
  - `kubectl -n optimize get pods -l app=optimize-suggestions-service`
- Recent errors:
  - `kubectl -n optimize logs deploy/optimize-suggestions-service --since=10m | grep -E "ERROR|panic|timeout"`

---

## Appendix: Known Edge Cases
- Large orgs with many routes/models can produce heavy list queries if filtering is absent.
- Certain sorts (e.g., sorting by computed savings fields) can be expensive if not indexed.
- Page token parsing errors can cascade into 5xx if not handled defensively.
- Retries from Console can amplify load; watch for sudden RPS step functions correlated with UI changes.

If any of the above is suspected, prefer mitigation via throttling + rollback, then follow up with indexing/query fixes and contract-safe caching.

title: Runbook: One-click rollback for Optimization Config (Safe Apply)
preview:
## Purpose
This runbook describes how to execute a **one-click rollback** of an Optimization Config version (batching, caching, and quantization profiles) for Hosted and Dedicated deployments, and how to validate recovery.

Rollback is intended to be:
- **Fast** (single action from Console or API)
- **Safe** (idempotent + race-condition guarded)
- **Auditable** (structured audit events)
- **Scoped** (tenant/model/region aware)

## Audience
- SRE on-call
- Eng Platform (Control Plane) on-call
- Runtime on-call
- Console on-call

## Definitions
- **Optimization Config**: Serving-time knobs (batching heuristics, prefix/KV cache toggles, quantization profile selection) applied by the runtime.
- **Config Version**: Immutable, versioned object (e.g., `ocv_2026_02_24_0017`).
- **Active Version**: The version currently pinned for a given scope (tenant/model/region).
- **Apply / Rollout**: Staged propagation of an active version to workers.
- **Rollback**: Re-point the active version to a prior known-good version and propagate.

## When to rollback (triggers)
Rollback is appropriate when a config change is the most likely cause of a regression and mitigation speed is required.

Common triggers:
1. **Elevated error rate**
   - 5xx rate increase correlated with an Optimization Config apply event.
   - Error types indicating capacity/timeout (e.g., `gateway_timeout`, `runtime_overloaded`, `cuda_oom`).
2. **Latency regression**
   - p95 or p99 latency breach correlated with apply event.
   - Streaming stalls / first-token latency spikes.
3. **Throughput or saturation regressions**
   - Sudden drop in tokens/sec or requests/sec.
   - GPU utilization pinned high with increased queue time.
4. **Quality regression (quantization only)**
   - Evals gate failure (e.g., win-rate drop, task score regression) after a quantization profile promotion.
5. **Incorrect scope / mis-apply**
   - Config applied to wrong region/model/tenant (e.g., cache toggle intended for `us-east` landed in `eu-west`).

## Decision criteria (how to decide quickly)
Use the table below as guidance. When in doubt during a Sev event, bias toward rollback if:
- the change was recent (≤ 60 minutes), and
- the regression aligns with the config’s blast radius, and
- there is a known-good prior version.

### Decision table
- Roll back immediately if any of the following are true:
  1) **5xx rate** is above the scope SLO threshold for **10 minutes** AND the regression began after the apply event.
  2) **p95 latency** is above the scope SLO threshold for **15 minutes** AND there is no concurrent infra incident.
  3) **Quant quality gate** failed with high confidence (per Evals harness) AND the active quant profile was promoted in the last 24 hours.
  4) A scoping mistake is confirmed (wrong tenant/model/region).

- Consider pausing rollout (instead of rollback) if:
  - the rollout is still in early canary and metrics are noisy but trending within bounds, OR
  - there is an ongoing regional capacity incident unrelated to config.

### Default SLO thresholds for gating/rollback decisions (unless tenant override exists)
These are the defaults used by the rollout controller and referenced in incident decisions:
- **5xx rate**: > 0.8% for 10 minutes (scope: tenant+model+region)
- **p95 latency**: > 1.20x baseline for 15 minutes (baseline = previous 6h median for same scope)
- **p99 latency**: > 1.35x baseline for 15 minutes
- **Saturation guardrail**: queue time p95 > 250ms for 10 minutes OR GPU util > 92% with increasing queue depth

Notes:
- For Dedicated clusters, compare to that tenant’s historical baselines; do not compare to global Hosted baseline.
- For Private, see “Private deployment rollback” section.

## Preconditions / permissions
1. You must have one of the following RBAC roles:
   - `optimization-change-operator` (execute rollback within approved scopes)
   - `optimization-admin` (override/cross-scope rollback; requires change ticket)

2. Confirm you are acting on the correct **scope**:
   - Tenant: `tenant_id`
   - Model: `model_id` (or model family)
   - Region: `region`

3. Confirm you have the “known-good” target version:
   - Typically the last promoted version before the current change.
   - If uncertain, select the last version marked `promoted` with stable metrics (see Console timeline).

## Safety notes (do not skip)
- **Rollback is idempotent**: re-sending the same rollback request should be safe.
- **Concurrency guard**: if an apply is currently progressing, rollback will either:
  - pause the rollout and proceed, or
  - fail with `409 CONFLICT` if the rollout is in a non-interruptible checkpoint (rare). Follow the “Rollback blocked by in-flight apply” procedure.
- **Blast radius**: rollback affects the full selected scope; verify tenant/model/region carefully.
- **Cache invalidation**: some config fields (cache toggles / KV cache strategy) can cause short transient latency changes during reconfiguration. Expect a brief bump; monitor for recovery.

## Primary procedure (Console one-click rollback)
### Step 0 — Confirm correlation and gather identifiers
1. Open **Optimization Rollout Health Dashboard** and filter to the suspected scope:
   - `tenant_id`, `model_id`, `region`
2. Check chart annotations for:
   - `optimization_config.apply.started`
   - `optimization_config.promoted`
   - `optimization_config.rollback.started`
3. In Console, navigate to:
   - **Console → Optimize → Optimization Config → History**
4. Copy:
   - Current active version: `active_version_id`
   - Candidate rollback target version: `target_version_id`
   - Most recent rollout id (if shown): `rollout_id`

### Step 1 — Validate target version and scope
In the History timeline:
- Ensure `target_version_id` is marked **Promoted** or “Known Good” (internal tag), OR previously ran stable in production.
- Confirm the scope shown in the right rail matches the incident scope:
  - Tenant, model, region, and environment (Hosted vs Dedicated)

### Step 2 — Initiate rollback
1. Click **Rollback** next to the currently active version.
2. In the confirmation modal:
   - Verify **blast radius summary** (tenant/model/region)
   - Verify **from → to** versions
   - Review any warnings (e.g., “rollout is partially applied”, “cooldown active”)
   - Enter the required confirmation text (type-to-confirm)
3. Select a reason code:
   - `latency_regression`, `error_rate_regression`, `quality_regression`, `scope_misapply`, `other`
4. Click **Confirm rollback**.

Expected results:
- Rollback status changes to **In Progress** within ~10s.
- You receive a rollback request id: `rollback_id`.

### Step 3 — Monitor rollback progression
1. Watch the **Rollback Progress** panel:
   - Percentage of workers updated
   - Stragglers (by availability zone / worker pool)
2. Monitor key metrics for the scope:
   - 5xx rate
   - p95/p99 latency
   - Throughput (req/s, tokens/s)
   - GPU utilization and queue time

Expected rollback completion:
- Hosted: typically 2–8 minutes depending on worker pool size.
- Dedicated: typically 3–12 minutes depending on cluster size and rollout policy.

### Step 4 — Verify stabilization
Proceed to the verification checklist below (do not declare “mitigated” until checks pass).

## Alternate procedure (API rollback)
Use the API if Console is unavailable, during automation, or for advanced debugging.

### Required inputs
- `tenant_id`, `model_id`, `region`
- `target_version_id` (known-good)
- `reason` (string)

### Example (curl)
1. Obtain an auth token with required RBAC.
2. Trigger rollback:

POST https://control-plane.redwoodinference.com/v1/optimization-config/rollbacks
Headers:
- Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN
- Content-Type: application/json

Body:
{
  "tenant_id": "tnt_123",
  "model_id": "rw-chat-13b",
  "region": "us-east-1",
  "target_version_id": "ocv_2026_02_12_0009",
  "reason": "latency_regression",
  "incident_id": "INC-1842",
  "dry_run": false
}

Expected response:
- `202 Accepted`
- includes `rollback_id` and current `state`.

### Check rollback status
GET https://control-plane.redwoodinference.com/v1/optimization-config/rollbacks/{rollback_id}

If status is `blocked` or `conflict`, follow the troubleshooting section.

## Post-rollback verification checklist
### A. Control plane / config correctness
- [ ] Console shows the scope’s **Active Version** = `target_version_id`.
- [ ] The rollback timeline entry shows `started_at` and `completed_at` (or stable progress if still running).
- [ ] The rollout controller is not advancing the previous version (no lingering “apply in progress” for the old version).

### B. Runtime propagation
- [ ] Worker compliance ≥ 99% (or per Dedicated policy) within 15 minutes.
- [ ] No sustained stragglers; investigate if a specific pool/zone is stuck.

### C. User-facing health
Check for at least 10–15 minutes after rollback completion:
- [ ] 5xx rate returns to baseline and below threshold.
- [ ] p95 and p99 latency recover to within expected band.
- [ ] Throughput recovers (req/s, tokens/s) and queue time decreases.

### D. Quality checks (quantization rollbacks)
If rollback reverted a quantization profile:
- [ ] Evals harness shows gate restored to pass state (or no longer failing).
- [ ] For high-impact tenants, run the quick eval pack (if available) against the reverted version.

### E. Audit / incident hygiene
- [ ] Audit event emitted for rollback (see “Audit events” below).
- [ ] Incident channel updated with:
  - scope, from→to versions, rollback_id
  - observed recovery metrics
- [ ] Create follow-up ticket to investigate root cause of regression.

## Troubleshooting / edge cases
### 1) Rollback blocked by in-flight apply (409 CONFLICT)
Symptoms:
- API/Console returns conflict indicating an apply is at a checkpoint.

Actions:
1. In Console, click **Pause rollout** for the in-flight apply (if available).
2. Wait 60–120 seconds and retry rollback.
3. If still blocked:
   - Page Eng Platform on-call (Control Plane)
   - Page Runtime on-call if worker state appears mixed

Do NOT attempt manual worker restarts unless directed by Runtime/SRE; it can worsen mixed-state recovery.

### 2) Rollback initiated but metrics do not recover
Actions:
1. Confirm rollback actually propagated (compliance ≥ 99%).
2. Check if another change occurred concurrently (runtime deploy, autoscaling, regional incident).
3. If no recovery and compliance is high:
   - The regression may not be caused by Optimization Config.
   - Escalate to Runtime/SRE to investigate capacity, kernel changes, or upstream dependencies.
4. Consider rolling back other recent changes per standard change management policy.

### 3) Mixed worker states persist (partial apply/rollback)
Symptoms:
- Compliance oscillates; some workers report old version.

Actions:
1. Confirm the runtime is receiving the active version (control plane “active pointer” is correct).
2. Check worker logs for config fetch failures or cache invalidation failures.
3. Engage Runtime on-call; they may:
   - trigger targeted resync
   - drain and recycle a subset of workers if safe

### 4) Wrong-scope rollback executed
Actions (time critical):
1. Immediately roll forward to the prior active version for the mistakenly affected scope (reverse the rollback) or pin the correct version.
2. Notify Security/Compliance if customer impact occurred.
3. File incident follow-up and add scope guardrail feedback to Console team.

## Audit events (what should be emitted)
Rollback should emit the following audit events (names may appear in SIEM exports):
- `optimization_config.rollback.requested`
- `optimization_config.rollback.started`
- `optimization_config.rollback.completed`
- `optimization_config.rollback.failed` (if applicable)

Minimum expected fields in the event payload:
- `actor_id`, `actor_email` (or service principal)
- `tenant_id`, `model_id`, `region`
- `from_version_id`, `to_version_id`
- `rollback_id`, `request_id`, `trace_id`
- `reason`, `incident_id` (if provided)

If audit events are missing, file a Security issue and reference the rollback_id and request_id.

## Communications (incident channel template)
Copy/paste:
- Scope: tenant={TENANT} model={MODEL} region={REGION}
- Action: rollback optimization config {FROM_VERSION} → {TO_VERSION}
- rollback_id: {ROLLBACK_ID}
- Reason: {REASON}
- Expected: recovery within 5–15 min post-completion; watching 5xx + p95/p99

## Private deployment rollback (VPC/on-prem)
For Private deployments, the control plane versioning model is the same, but the operator workflow depends on installation method.

High-level guidance:
1. Identify current and target Optimization Config versions.
2. Pin the target version using the supported mechanism (Helm values / installer / Terraform).
3. Apply the change and monitor the customer’s dashboards.

Authoritative steps are in:
- confluence://eng-private-deployments/upgrade-and-rollback/private-deployment-optimization-config-rollback

Escalation:
- If the customer is air-gapped or has strict maintenance windows, coordinate with Solutions Engineering and Customer Success before proceeding.

## Escalation policy
- Sev1/Sev2 impacting multiple tenants/regions:
  - Page SRE on-call + Eng Platform on-call immediately.
- If rollback is blocked or mixed-state persists > 15 minutes:
  - Page Runtime on-call.
- If auditability/RBAC issues prevent rollback:
  - Page Security on-call (product security) and use break-glass process per policy.

## Appendix: Quick checks
### Identify active version quickly
- Console: Optimize → Optimization Config → “Active” badge for the selected scope.
- Response header (if enabled for the route): `x-redwood-optimization-config-version: <version>`

### Common root causes this rollback mitigates
- Batching heuristic change causing increased queue time under certain prompt length distributions
- Cache toggle misconfigured for a region with different traffic shape
- Quantization profile promotion causing quality regression or higher latency due to kernel path mismatch
- Semantic validation gap (config “valid” but unsafe for a specific model variant)

## Change log
- 2026-02-24: Updated thresholds section and added concurrency conflict procedure based on internal beta gameday learnings.
- 2026-02-03: Added Private deployment section and audit event field expectations.
- 2026-01-18: Initial published runbook for internal beta.

   - GPU utilization pinned high with increased queue time.
4. **Quality regression (quantization only)**
   - Evals gate failure (e.g., win-rate drop, task score regression) after a quantization profile promotion.
5. **Incorrect scope / mis-apply**
   - Config applied to wrong region/model/tenant (e.g., cache toggle intended for `us-east` landed in `eu-west`).

## Decision criteria (how to decide quickly)
Use the table below as guidance. When in doubt during a Sev event, bias toward rollback if:
- the change was recent (≤ 60 minutes), and
- the regression aligns with the config’s blast radius, and
- there is a known-good prior version.

### Decision table
- Roll back immediately if any of the following are true:
  1) **5xx rate** is above the scope SLO threshold for **10 minutes** AND the regression began after the apply event.
  2) **p95 latency** is above the scope SLO threshold for **15 minutes** AND there is no concurrent infra incident.
  3) **Quant quality gate** failed with high confidence (per Evals harness) AND the active quant profile was promoted in the last 24 hours.
  4) A scoping mistake is confirmed (wrong tenant/model/region).

- Consider pausing rollout (instead of rollback) if:
  - the rollout is still in early canary and metrics are noisy but trending within bounds, OR
  - there is an ongoing regional capacity incident unrelated to config.

### Default SLO thresholds for gating/rollback decisions (unless tenant override exists)
These are the defaults used by the rollout controller and referenced in incident decisions:
- **5xx rate**: > 0.8% for 10 minutes (scope: tenant+model+region)
- **p95 latency**: > 1.20x baseline for 15 minutes (baseline = previous 6h median for same scope)
- **p99 latency**: > 1.35x baseline for 15 minutes
- **Saturation guardrail**: queue time p95 > 250ms for 10 minutes OR GPU util > 92% with increasing queue depth

Notes:
- For Dedicated clusters, compare to that tenant’s historical baselines; do not compare to global Hosted baseline.
- For Private, see “Private deployment rollback” section.

## Preconditions / permissions
1. You must have one of the following RBAC roles:
   - `optimization-change-operator` (execute rollback within approved scopes)
   - `optimization-admin` (override/cross-scope rollback; requires change ticket)

2. Confirm you are acting on the correct **scope**:
   - Tenant: `tenant_id`
   - Model: `model_id` (or model family)
   - Region: `region`

3. Confirm you have the “known-good” target version:
   - Typically the last promoted version before the current change.
   - If uncertain, select the last version marked `promoted` with stable metrics (see Console timeline).

## Safety notes (do not skip)
- **Rollback is idempotent**: re-sending the same rollback request should be safe.
- **Concurrency guard**: if an apply is currently progressing, rollback will either:
  - pause the rollout and proceed, or
  - fail with `409 CONFLICT` if the rollout is in a non-interruptible checkpoint (rare). Follow the “Rollback blocked by in-flight apply” procedure.
- **Blast radius**: rollback affects the full selected scope; verify tenant/model/region carefully.
- **Cache invalidation**: some config fields (cache toggles / KV cache strategy) can cause short transient latency changes during reconfiguration. Expect a brief bump; monitor for recovery.

## Primary procedure (Console one-click rollback)
### Step 0 — Confirm correlation and gather identifiers
1. Open **Optimization Rollout Health Dashboard** and filter to the suspected scope:
   - `tenant_id`, `model_id`, `region`
2. Check chart annotations for:
   - `optimization_config.apply.started`
   - `optimization_config.promoted`
   - `optimization_config.rollback.started`
3. In Console, navigate to:
   - **Console → Optimize → Optimization Config → History**
4. Copy:
   - Current active version: `active_version_id`
   - Candidate rollback target version: `target_version_id`
   - Most recent rollout id (if shown): `rollout_id`

### Step 1 — Validate target version and scope
In the History timeline:
- Ensure `target_version_id` is marked **Promoted** or “Known Good” (internal tag), OR previously ran stable in production.
- Confirm the scope shown in the right rail matches the incident scope:
  - Tenant, model, region, and environment (Hosted vs Dedicated)

### Step 2 — Initiate rollback
1. Click **Rollback** next to the currently active version.
2. In the confirmation modal:
   - Verify **blast radius summary** (tenant/model/region)
   - Verify **from → to** versions
   - Review any warnings (e.g., “rollout is partially applied”, “cooldown active”)
   - Enter the required confirmation text (type-to-confirm)
3. Select a reason code:
   - `latency_regression`, `error_rate_regression`, `quality_regression`, `scope_misapply`, `other`
4. Click **Confirm rollback**.

Expected results:
- Rollback status changes to **In Progress** within ~10s.
- You receive a rollback request id: `rollback_id`.

### Step 3 — Monitor rollback progression
1. Watch the **Rollback Progress** panel:
   - Percentage of workers updated
   - Stragglers (by availability zone / worker pool)
2. Monitor key metrics for the scope:
   - 5xx rate
   - p95/p99 latency
   - Throughput (req/s, tokens/s)
   - GPU utilization and queue time

Expected rollback completion:
- Hosted: typically 2–8 minutes depending on worker pool size.
- Dedicated: typically 3–12 minutes depending on cluster size and rollout policy.

### Step 4 — Verify stabilization
Proceed to the verification checklist below (do not declare “mitigated” until checks pass).

## Alternate procedure (API rollback)
Use the API if Console is unavailable, during automation, or for advanced debugging.

### Required inputs
- `tenant_id`, `model_id`, `region`
- `target_version_id` (known-good)
- `reason` (string)

### Example (curl)
1. Obtain an auth token with required RBAC.
2. Trigger rollback:

POST https://control-plane.redwoodinference.com/v1/optimization-config/rollbacks
Headers:
- Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN
- Content-Type: application/json

Body:
{
  "tenant_id": "tnt_123",
  "model_id": "rw-chat-13b",
  "region": "us-east-1",
  "target_version_id": "ocv_2026_02_12_0009",
  "reason": "latency_regression",
  "incident_id": "INC-1842",
  "dry_run": false
}

Expected response:
- `202 Accepted`
- includes `rollback_id` and current `state`.

### Check rollback status
GET https://control-plane.redwoodinference.com/v1/optimization-config/rollbacks/{rollback_id}

If status is `blocked` or `conflict`, follow the troubleshooting section.

## Post-rollback verification checklist
### A. Control plane / config correctness
- [ ] Console shows the scope’s **Active Version** = `target_version_id`.
- [ ] The rollback timeline entry shows `started_at` and `completed_at` (or stable progress if still running).
- [ ] The rollout controller is not advancing the previous version (no lingering “apply in progress” for the old version).

### B. Runtime propagation
- [ ] Worker compliance ≥ 99% (or per Dedicated policy) within 15 minutes.
- [ ] No sustained stragglers; investigate if a specific pool/zone is stuck.

### C. User-facing health
Check for at least 10–15 minutes after rollback completion:
- [ ] 5xx rate returns to baseline and below threshold.
- [ ] p95 and p99 latency recover to within expected band.
- [ ] Throughput recovers (req/s, tokens/s) and queue time decreases.

### D. Quality checks (quantization rollbacks)
If rollback reverted a quantization profile:
- [ ] Evals harness shows gate restored to pass state (or no longer failing).
- [ ] For high-impact tenants, run the quick eval pack (if available) against the reverted version.

### E. Audit / incident hygiene
- [ ] Audit event emitted for rollback (see “Audit events” below).
- [ ] Incident channel updated with:
  - scope, from→to versions, rollback_id
  - observed recovery metrics
- [ ] Create follow-up ticket to investigate root cause of regression.

## Troubleshooting / edge cases
### 1) Rollback blocked by in-flight apply (409 CONFLICT)
Symptoms:
- API/Console returns conflict indicating an apply is at a checkpoint.

Actions:
1. In Console, click **Pause rollout** for the in-flight apply (if available).
2. Wait 60–120 seconds and retry rollback.
3. If still blocked:
   - Page Eng Platform on-call (Control Plane)
   - Page Runtime on-call if worker state appears mixed

Do NOT attempt manual worker restarts unless directed by Runtime/SRE; it can worsen mixed-state recovery.

### 2) Rollback initiated but metrics do not recover
Actions:
1. Confirm rollback actually propagated (compliance ≥ 99%).
2. Check if another change occurred concurrently (runtime deploy, autoscaling, regional incident).
3. If no recovery and compliance is high:
   - The regression may not be caused by Optimization Config.
   - Escalate to Runtime/SRE to investigate capacity, kernel changes, or upstream dependencies.
4. Consider rolling back other recent changes per standard change management policy.

### 3) Mixed worker states persist (partial apply/rollback)
Symptoms:
- Compliance oscillates; some workers report old version.

Actions:
1. Confirm the runtime is receiving the active version (control plane “active pointer” is correct).
2. Check worker logs for config fetch failures or cache invalidation failures.
3. Engage Runtime on-call; they may:
   - trigger targeted resync
   - drain and recycle a subset of workers if safe

### 4) Wrong-scope rollback executed
Actions (time critical):
1. Immediately roll forward to the prior active version for the mistakenly affected scope (reverse the rollback) or pin the correct version.
2. Notify Security/Compliance if customer impact occurred.
3. File incident follow-up and add scope guardrail feedback to Console team.

## Audit events (what should be emitted)
Rollback should emit the following audit events (names may appear in SIEM exports):
- `optimization_config.rollback.requested`
- `optimization_config.rollback.started`
- `optimization_config.rollback.completed`
- `optimization_config.rollback.failed` (if applicable)

Minimum expected fields in the event payload:
- `actor_id`, `actor_email` (or service principal)
- `tenant_id`, `model_id`, `region`
- `from_version_id`, `to_version_id`
- `rollback_id`, `request_id`, `trace_id`
- `reason`, `incident_id` (if provided)

If audit events are missing, file a Security issue and reference the rollback_id and request_id.

## Communications (incident channel template)
Copy/paste:
- Scope: tenant={TENANT} model={MODEL} region={REGION}
- Action: rollback optimization config {FROM_VERSION} → {TO_VERSION}
- rollback_id: {ROLLBACK_ID}
- Reason: {REASON}
- Expected: recovery within 5–15 min post-completion; watching 5xx + p95/p99

## Private deployment rollback (VPC/on-prem)
For Private deployments, the control plane versioning model is the same, but the operator workflow depends on installation method.

High-level guidance:
1. Identify current and target Optimization Config versions.
2. Pin the target version using the supported mechanism (Helm values / installer / Terraform).
3. Apply the change and monitor the customer’s dashboards.

Authoritative steps are in:
- confluence://eng-private-deployments/upgrade-and-rollback/private-deployment-optimization-config-rollback

Escalation:
- If the customer is air-gapped or has strict maintenance windows, coordinate with Solutions Engineering and Customer Success before proceeding.

## Escalation policy
- Sev1/Sev2 impacting multiple tenants/regions:
  - Page SRE on-call + Eng Platform on-call immediately.
- If rollback is blocked or mixed-state persists > 15 minutes:
  - Page Runtime on-call.
- If auditability/RBAC issues prevent rollback:
  - Page Security on-call (product security) and use break-glass process per policy.

## Appendix: Quick checks
### Identify active version quickly
- Console: Optimize → Optimization Config → “Active” badge for the selected scope.
- Response header (if enabled for the route): `x-redwood-optimization-config-version: <version>`

### Common root causes this rollback mitigates
- Batching heuristic change causing increased queue time under certain prompt length distributions
- Cache toggle misconfigured for a region with different traffic shape
- Quantization profile promotion causing quality regression or higher latency due to kernel path mismatch
- Semantic validation gap (config “valid” but unsafe for a specific model variant)

## Change log
- 2026-02-24: Updated thresholds section and added concurrency conflict procedure based on internal beta gameday learnings.
- 2026-02-03: Added Private deployment section and audit event field expectations.
- 2026-01-18: Initial published runbook for internal beta.

title: How to enable the Optimization Suggestions beta per org (feature flag)
preview:
## Purpose
This page explains how to enable the **Optimization Suggestions (batching/caching) beta** for a specific customer org using feature flags.

This is an internal-only operational procedure used by Product/CS/Solutions and on-call engineering during beta rollout.

---

## What gets enabled
The beta is gated by **two controls**:

1) **Console UI flag** (shows the Suggestions entry point, list view, and details drawer)
- Flag key: `console.optimize_suggestions_beta`

2) **Backend API allowlist flag** (permits `/optimize/suggestions` reads for the org)
- Flag key: `optimize.suggestions_api_enabled`

Both flags must be enabled for the org to have a functional experience.

Why two flags?
- The API flag prevents accidental exposure via direct API calls.
- The UI flag allows gradual UX rollout and easy UI-only rollback without fully disabling the service.

---

## Ownership and approvals
**Owners**
- Console UX: Dylan Brooks / Marissa Cole
- Backend/RBAC: Chloe Martin / Kaitlyn Nguyen
- Telemetry readiness: Logan Wright / Nadia Rahman

**Approval requirement (beta)**
- At minimum: Product lead (Marissa or Aditya) + Engineering on-call (eng-platform) for the enabling action.
- For regulated/enterprise orgs: add Security review sign-off (Marcus Lin) if the customer requires additional audit evidence.

---

## Preconditions (must be true before enabling)
Use this checklist before toggling flags:

### A) RBAC / permissions
- The org has at least one user with the permission scope: `optimize:suggestions:read`.
- If the customer reports “page exists but empty/denied,” check RBAC first.

### B) Telemetry readiness
The suggestions pipeline requires sufficient telemetry to compute estimates.
- Traffic: at least one eligible route with sustained requests (rule of thumb: \~1k+ requests/day over the last 7 days).
- Telemetry: request metrics and cost attribution signals present (route/model, token counts, latency distributions, cache metrics where applicable).

If telemetry is missing, enabling the UI will likely show an “Insufficient telemetry” empty state.

### C) Customer eligibility (Product)
Confirm the org matches beta criteria (telemetry maturity + cost sensitivity + willingness to provide feedback). See the beta target list owned by PM/CS.

---

## Where to make the change
Redwood uses **Switchboard** (internal feature flag service) with:
- Web UI: Switchboard Console
- CLI: `rwd switchboard`

Prefer the CLI for auditable, copy/pasteable changes, but UI is acceptable if you complete the audit fields.

---

## Procedure: enable Suggestions beta for a single org
### Step 1 — Gather required inputs
Collect the following:
- Org ID (UUID) and Org name
- Requesting stakeholder (PM/CS owner)
- Enablement reason (e.g., “Private beta cohort #2”)
- Ticket reference (Linear/Jira/HubSpot) for traceability

### Step 2 — Check current flag state
Using CLI:

`rwd switchboard flags get console.optimize_suggestions_beta --org <ORG_ID>`

`rwd switchboard flags get optimize.suggestions_api_enabled --org <ORG_ID>`

Expected output should clearly indicate `on/off` plus last actor.

### Step 3 — Enable backend API allowlist flag
Enable the API first to avoid a UI that renders but can’t fetch.

`rwd switchboard flags set optimize.suggestions_api_enabled --org <ORG_ID> --on \
  --reason "Suggestions beta enablement" \
  --ticket "PM-857" \
  --expires "2026-05-01"`

Notes:
- Always include `--ticket` and `--reason`.
- Use `--expires` for beta flags so we have an automatic cleanup point.

### Step 4 — Enable Console UI flag

`rwd switchboard flags set console.optimize_suggestions_beta --org <ORG_ID> --on \
  --reason "Suggestions beta enablement" \
  --ticket "PM-857" \
  --expires "2026-05-01"`

### Step 5 — Verify access and UX behavior
Verify in this order:
1) **API permission**: impersonate an org admin (or use a test user in the org) and call:
   - `GET /optimize/suggestions?project_id=<PROJECT_ID>&limit=25`
   Expected: `200` with an array (may be empty) and `stale_as_of` if present.
2) **Console**: navigate to Console → Optimize → Suggestions.
3) Confirm the page shows one of:
   - Suggestion list (ideal)
   - Empty state: “Insufficient telemetry” (acceptable if expected)
   - Empty state: “No suggestions right now” (acceptable)

### Step 6 — Confirm audit logging and notifications
Within 5 minutes, confirm:
- Switchboard audit log has entries for both flag toggles.
- The `#eng-platform-flag-changes` Slack channel shows an automated notification.

If either is missing, treat as an operational issue; open an eng-platform incident if multiple changes are not being audited.

---

## Audit and logging expectations (required)
Every enable/disable action must be traceable.

### Required fields
When toggling any beta flag, record:
- Actor (must be a named Redwood employee account; no shared accounts)
- Org ID
- Flag key and new value
- Reason
- Ticket/reference
- Expiration date (beta only)

### Where the audit trail lives
- Switchboard audit log: `FeatureFlagChange` events
- Data warehouse: `audit.feature_flag_changes` (hourly export)
- Slack: `#eng-platform-flag-changes` (best-effort notification)

### Do NOT include customer data
Do not paste customer prompts, payloads, or request bodies into the reason field.

---

## Rollback procedure
Rollback should be fast and reversible. Prefer UI rollback first unless there is an API/security issue.

### When to rollback
Rollback if any of the following occur:
- Suggestions API p95 latency regression attributable to the org (or global) and mitigations aren’t sufficient.
- Elevated 5xx rates or repeated timeouts.
- Data correctness issue that materially misleads (e.g., obviously inflated $/day estimates confirmed by investigation).
- Access control/RBAC issue with risk of cross-tenant exposure (immediate rollback; escalate to Security).

### Rollback steps
1) Disable Console UI flag:

`rwd switchboard flags set console.optimize_suggestions_beta --org <ORG_ID> --off \
  --reason "Rollback Suggestions beta" \
  --ticket "ENG-<ID or incident>"`

2) If needed (API issue), disable API flag:

`rwd switchboard flags set optimize.suggestions_api_enabled --org <ORG_ID> --off \
  --reason "Rollback Suggestions beta" \
  --ticket "ENG-<ID or incident>"`

3) Verify:
- Console no longer shows Suggestions entry point for the org.
- API returns `403` (or `404` depending on gateway behavior) for org-scoped requests.

4) Notify stakeholders:
- Post in `#product-optimize` and `#eng-platform` with org name, reason, ticket, and next steps.

---

## Troubleshooting
### “Suggestions page is empty but traffic is high”
Common causes:
- Telemetry pipeline lag/backfill delay
- Missing cost attribution fields for that project
- Org is enabled but project-scoped query has no eligible routes

Actions:
- Check telemetry freshness and compute lag dashboards.
- Confirm the org/project has events in the last 24h.
- If compute lag is present, follow the Suggestions API latency/error triage runbook.

### “User cannot access Suggestions (RBAC denied)”
- Confirm the user has `optimize:suggestions:read`.
- Check recent RBAC changes and audit log.
- If support needs to request access changes, route through the internal helpdesk workflow.

### “Estimates don’t match billing”
- Remind: estimates are directional and based on recent telemetry windows.
- Verify the time window shown in the UI and the estimator assumptions.
- Escalate to Applied ML/Optimize only after verifying the org has correct input signals.

---

## Appendix: Flag keys and notes
| Flag key | Scope | Default | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| `console.optimize_suggestions_beta` | org | OFF | Shows UI surfaces in Console |
| `optimize.suggestions_api_enabled` | org | OFF | Allows `/optimize/suggestions` reads |

Beta hygiene:
- Always set an expiration date.
- Review enabled orgs weekly during beta.
- Remove/replace flags as part of GA readiness work (per PM-857).
